data.title: Apple works on new feature allowing Siri to tackle multiple tasks at once: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) is working on a new feature for an updated version of Siri that would allow the digital assistant to handle multiple tasks from a single prompt, according to Bloomberg.

The feature could be released when Apple unveils its iOS 27, iPadOS 27, and macOS 27 operating systems later this year, the report said, citing people familiar with the development. This would allow a user to ask Siri to play music, send a text message, and make a phone call in a single request. Currently, users have to make a separate request for each action.

The update would put Siri more in line with the capabilities of large language model chatbots such as OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT, Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Gemini, and Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude.

Apple is expected to highlight updates to Siri during its Worldwide Developers Conference on June 8.

data.title: Citi upgrades European defense names, sees limited upside for Rheinmetall
data.description:
Citi on Tuesday upgraded several Western European aerospace and defense stocks, arguing that recent share price movements have created more attractive entry points, even as parts of the sector may be nearing peak demand.

In a March 31 research note, analysts led by Charles Armitage raised their ratings on Babcock International (BCKIF)(BCKIY), Leonardo (FNMBF)(FNMBY) and Thyssenkrupp Marine Systems (TKMSF) to Buy, citing improved earnings visibility and supportive long-term defense spending trends.

Citi set target prices of £15.54 for Babcock, €69 for Leonardo and €100 for TKMS, implying meaningful upside from current levels.

Growth Expectations Anchored in Defense Spending

The upgrades are underpinned by expectations that European governments will sustain elevated defense budgets through the next decade. Citi’s analysis, based on Bloomberg consensus estimates through 2030, suggests:

  • Babcock could deliver roughly 5% compound annual earnings growth into the mid-2030s
  • Leonardo is projected to achieve about 5% CAGR
  • TKMS is also expected to grow earnings at around 5% annually

However, the report notes that investor expectations may already be pricing in stronger growth for certain companies. In particular, Citi highlighted Saab (SAABF) (SAABY) as a name where the market appears to assume significantly higher profit expansion, potentially outpacing procurement budget growth.

Rheinmetall initiated with Neutral

Citi also initiated coverage on Rheinmetall (RNMBF)(RNMBY) with a Neutral rating and a €1,480 price target.

While acknowledging Rheinmetall’s strong positioning in munitions and land systems, analysts cautioned that current demand for weapons and ammunition may be unsustainably high. They expect utilization rates, currently elevated due to replenishment of depleted stockpiles, to normalize over time, potentially falling back to 5% to 6% of capacity once inventories are rebuilt.

What’s already priced in

A key focus of the report is what the market is already discounting. Citi’s analysis compares implied profit growth through 2035 against expected increases in procurement budgets, highlighting where expectations may be stretched.

The firm suggests that while the sector continues to benefit from geopolitical tensions and rearmament across Europe, not all stocks offer equal risk-reward profiles at current valuations.

Citi’s call reflects a more selective approach to the European defense sector. While structural tailwinds remain intact, the firm sees better value in names like Babcock, Leonardo and TKMS, where growth expectations appear more aligned with realistic spending trajectories.

At the same time, the report signals caution on areas where demand could normalize, reinforcing the view that even in a strong cycle, not all defense stocks will rise equally.

data.title: The oil markets are experiencing a structural shift – Energy Aspects
data.description:
Oil markets are undergoing a structural shift that will push crude (CO1:COM) prices to a bare minimum of $70 to $80 per barrel once conditions stabilize, according to Amrita Sen, founder and director of market intelligence at Energy Aspects.

Sen told CNBC that despite recent sharp sell-offs, the underlying fundamentals driving higher prices remain unchanged, with geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and production delays creating lasting impacts on global supply.

“What we are talking about is a structural shift,” Sen explained.

She noted that even if conflicts in the region were to end, it would take months for production to return to normal levels, while Asia is already experiencing “outright shortages.”

The strategic petroleum reserves being released now will eventually need to be replenished, adding significant restocking demand to an already tight market.

A key factor driving the structural shift is the changing investment calculus for energy companies considering Middle East projects.

“Every company I’m speaking to in Houston and in the U.S. is now reconsidering whether they want to invest in the Middle East,” Sen said. Companies now require a higher rate of return for projects in the region, effectively raising the breakeven price of oil by $10 to $15 per barrel.

The U.S. stands to benefit significantly from this realignment as Asian buyers seek more reliable crude sources. Sen pointed to a dramatic surge in U.S. exports, noting that shipments to Asia typically average around 1M barrels per day but are expected to exceed 2.1M barrels per day in April alone.

“The U.S. is absolutely the producer here [that is] benefiting,” she said, adding that midstream companies are also seeing gains as investment flows away from volatile regions.

However, Sen cautioned that infrastructure constraints could limit America’s ability to capitalize on this opportunity. While U.S. exports are expected to hit a record above 5M barrels per day, sustaining those levels will prove challenging.

“Vessel availability is going to be one of the biggest constraints in keeping crude oil exports out of the U.S. above 5M barrels per day,” she warned, noting that better pipeline connectivity to ports will also be needed.

Looking ahead, Sen suggested prices could climb even higher than her baseline estimates if upstream infrastructure has sustained damage during the current disruptions.

The shift represents a fundamental change in how global energy trade and investment are prioritized, with the Americas emerging as the preferred destination for both buyers seeking reliable supply and companies looking to de-risk their portfolios.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: NiSource started at Overweight by KeyBanc on 'doing data center growth the right way'
data.description:
NiSource (NI) wobbles between small gains and losses in Tuesday trading as KeyBanc initiates coverage with an Overweight rating and $52 price target, saying the utility is emerging as one of the primary beneficiaries of accelerating large load demand, particularly from data centers, and is well positioned to capture such growth without creating affordability concerns thanks to Indiana's supportive regulatory framework.

Constructive regulatory environments across its key jurisdictions, combined with relatively modest regulatory lag, should continue to support earnings growth and justify NiSource's (NI) position within the premium tier of regulated utilities over time, according to KeyBanc analyst Sophie Karp.

While NiSource (NI) shares have historically traded at a discount to peers, the stock trades at a ~1.5x premium, which Karp said is supported by the company's growth outlook and still offers room for further expansion.

NiSource (NI) also offers long-term upside through its exposure to data center development, Karp noted, with its service territory across the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic increasingly attractive to hyperscalers due to land availability, fiber access, and grid reliability; data centers require large, long-duration, and highly reliable electric loads, which align well with NiSource's regulated utility footprint and generation capabilities.

data.title: The recent pullback in stocks has no geopolitical connection, and it is not fundamentally driven – strategist
data.description:
The recent selling pressure in U.S. equity markets (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) has little connection to geopolitical tensions and is not fundamentally driven, according to Matthew Mishkin, co-chief investment strategist at Manulife John Hancock Investments.

In an interview with CNBC, Mishkin argued that “most of the selling pressure you’ve seen in the U.S. equity market has been driven from AI concerns or regulatory concerns or communication services or private credit and financial services.”

He added that high-quality tech stocks, despite being “beaten up the most,” have not been impacted by war-related factors.

Mishkin characterized the market downturn as a healthy correction following a momentum-driven rally earlier in the year.

“Some of this stuff had just rallied way too much year-to-date,” he explained, noting that the market needed “corrective price action” and “a resetting of sentiment because it was just getting a little too frothy.”

The strategist views this pullback as a natural unwinding of trend-following investment strategies that had dominated recent months.

The U.S. remains well-positioned to weather current challenges, according to Mishkin. He pointed to the country’s status as a net oil exporter with world-class natural gas reserves, along with technology and other industries that are not heavily reliant on energy resources.

“It’s not cheap by any means, but it’s cheaper than where we were just about a month ago,” he said of current market valuations.

With earnings season approaching, Mishkin advised investors to focus on fundamentals rather than geopolitical headlines. He emphasized the importance of identifying “companies that can drive earnings growth through this challenging macro environment.”

High-quality stocks with solid earnings potential represent an opportunity for investors seeking value during this period of uncertainty.

For a three-to-six-month outlook, Mishkin’s top recommendation centers on infrastructure-related equities, which include industrials (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ) and utilities (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU).

These sectors benefit from anticipated capital expenditure investment, offer dividend yields, and possess pricing power that provides defensive characteristics. The theme also encompasses aerospace and defense (ITA), (PPA), (XAR), (SHLD) companies.

Looking further ahead, Mishkin expressed confidence in quality tech stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW) despite their recent struggles.

He suggested that investors use any additional downside volatility as an opportunity to build positions in these names. With no fundamental reason behind the tech sector’s decline, Mishkin believes these stocks are positioned for an “upside resolution” once market conditions stabilize.

data.title: Auto demand remains solid amid geopolitical headwinds, buoyed by higher-income buyers
data.description:
JD Power presented its updated 2026 auto industry outlook today at the New York International Auto Show. One of the biggest takeaways from the industry research firm was that Americans are still buying cars, although the auto market is increasingly driven by buyers who can weather higher prices and gas costs. 

For the full year, JD Power maintained its forecast of 16.3M new-vehicle sales and pointed to continued strength in demand despite ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic volatility. 

One of the key themes from the JD Power forecast is that the auto industry continues to demonstrate increased resilience, supported by more adaptive planning, diversified supply strategies, and improved operational flexibility. Those capabilities were noted to have strengthened the industry’s ability to respond effectively to shifting conditions.

The firm warned that while demand remains strong, new vehicle affordability is still a barrier to higher vehicle sales. Average retail transaction prices are expected to rise 2.5% to $45,859 from a year ago. In aggregate, manufacturers’ incentive spend per vehicle is on track to reach $3,325, which is $165 higher than a year ago, suggesting that manufacturers still have more room to incentivize the purchase of new vehicles, per JD Power.

"There is no doubt that the current situation in the Middle East will make things more difficult to predict over the near term, and the longer-term risks to auto sales are material, but the fundamentals are strong for a solid year in 2026," previewed JD Power's Thomas King.

Looking ahead, the monthly and quarterly sales reports from automakers will be impacted by the comparison to a year ago that saw a surge of demand pulled forward by buyers to get ahead of the expiration of the EV tax credit on September 30, 2025.

Auto stocks: Toyota (TM), Tesla (TSLA), General Motors (GM), Honda (HMC), Ferrari (RACE), Ford (F), Hyundai (HYMTF), Nissan (NSANY), Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF), Volkswagen (VLKAF), BMW (BMWYY), Stellantis (STLA), Rivian Automotive (RIVN), and Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY).

data.title: Markets’ rally is an opportunity to look at value construct – Seymour Asset Management
data.description:
Tim Seymour, founder and CIO at Seymour Asset Management, believes the recent equity rally represents a legitimate market response to oversold conditions and attractive valuations heading into earnings season.

With the S&P 500 (SP500) now trading at approximately 19x forward earnings—down from 22x—Seymour sees genuine opportunity for investors willing to look past short-term volatility.

The S&P 500 (SP500) is up 2.6% on Tuesday, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is up 3.6%, and the Dow (DJI) is up 2.3%.

“There’s an opportunity to look at the value construct,” Seymour said in an interview with CNBC, noting that the market’s pullback from Liberation Day lows around 18x forward earnings has created favorable entry points.

He added that the VIX’s (VIX) recent movement has been “authoritative,” a significant shift from earlier rallies that weren’t confirmed by the volatility index.

For investors looking to put capital to work domestically, Seymour advocates a selective approach rather than broad market exposure. He favors integrated oil (USO), (UCO), (DBO) and energy stocks (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE) even if crude prices (CO1:COM) retreat, while pointing to utilities (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY) as both defensive plays and beneficiaries of data center demand.

Healthcare (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), he noted, has underperformed due to contagion from software sector (IGV), (IGPT), (XSW) weakness and presents an opportunity for patient investors.

Beyond U.S. borders, Seymour sees a secular shift underway that could benefit international markets for years to come. He argued that Europe (VGK), (IEV), (IEUR) currently offers a more favorable deregulation environment than the U.S. and is supported by deficit spending initiatives.

“A lot of the oxygen in the room was choked out” by the Mag Seven (MAGS) over the past five years, Seymour explained, leaving considerably more growth potential in international equities.

The case for international investing ultimately rests on fundamentals, according to Seymour, who manages the Amplify CWP International Enhanced Div Inc ETF (IDVO). Free cash flow generation and disciplined stock picking support the thesis, particularly if energy prices normalize.

He emphasized that this represents a “five-to-ten-year reallocation” rather than a short-term trade and stressed that long-term investors should take advantage of current conditions rather than waiting for a clear signal that the time is right to buy.

data.title: Nasdaq jumps 3.5% as these 10 stocks help drive the surge
data.description:
U.S. equities rallied sharply on Tuesday, led by a broad-based surge in technology shares as investor sentiment improved on rising expectations that tensions surrounding a potential Iran conflict could ease. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced 3.5% in afternoon trading, reflecting renewed risk appetite and strong participation across the market.

Market breadth underscored the strength of the move, with 82 of the Nasdaq 100’s (NDX) constituents trading higher on the session. The widespread gains suggest the rally was not concentrated in a small group of mega-cap names, but instead driven by broad investor demand across sectors.

Top-performing stocks within the Nasdaq 100 contributed meaningfully to the advance, highlighting robust momentum and reinforcing confidence that the rebound is being supported by a diverse set of companies. 

Outlined below are the Nasdaq-100’s (NDX) 10 best-performing stocks in Tuesday’s trading session. 

  1. Marvell Technology (MRVL) +12.98%
  2. Arm Holdings (ARM) +9.02%
  3. Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) +8.52%
  4. Microchip Technology (MCHP) +7.58%
  5. MercadoLibre (MELI) +6.66%
  6. Intel (INTC) +6.65% 
  7. Meta Platforms (META) +6.43%
  8. Shopify (SHOP) +6.41% 
  9. Seagate Technology (STX) +6.37% 
  10. Western Digital (WDC) +6.36% 

Nasdaq ETFs: (QQQ), (QQQM), (SQQQ), (TQQQ), (QLD), and (QID).

data.title: Charlotte's Web outlines Medicare pilot launch with up to $500 per beneficiary annually as BAT converts $55M debenture
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Charlotte's Web Holdings (CWEB:CA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Morachnick framed the quarter around a balance-sheet reset and Medicare channel entry: "this is not business as usual for Charlotte's Web" and said the company had "several key announcements that have tremendous positive impact on our business."
  • CEO Morachnick detailed the British American Tobacco transaction: "the conversion of BAT's outstanding $55 million convertible debenture, plus approximately $10 million in accrued interest" at "a conversion price of CAD 0.94 per share," plus "a new equity investment of $10 million through a private placement," adding that "BAT's combined equity commitment under this transaction is approximately $75 million" and that BAT "will hold approximately 40% of the company on a non-diluted basis."
  • CEO Morachnick positioned the CMMI Medicare pilot as a near-term operating catalyst, stating: "Participants in the ACO REACH Model and the Enhancing Oncology Model are anticipated to begin offering the Substance Access BEI beginning April 1, which is tomorrow" and emphasizing it as "an established health care integration pathway" with "CMS authorization, physician oversight... and structured outcomes data collection."
  • CFO Erika Lind tied results to restructuring and cost discipline: "our 2025 financial results reflects 2 years of disciplined execution to stabilize the business return to growth and fundamentally restructure our cost base to drive to profitability."
  • CEO Morachnick highlighted manufacturing compliance for healthcare-channel credibility: "this month Charlotte's Web completed its annual NSF dietary supplement good manufacturing practices audit and received zero findings."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide quantitative revenue or EPS guidance; CEO Morachnick described the CMMI pilot ramp as gradual: "I don't foresee massive revenue opportunity for, let's say, the balance of this year" and said adoption is expected to be "a gradual build over the next 12, 18 months."
  • CEO Morachnick described potential scale if Medicare pathways broaden: the pilot could extend within "the Medicare population, which currently includes approximately 67 million beneficiaries," and he added that "Medicare Advantage is about half of the 67 million participants in overall Medicare" while noting that "the timing of additional details for this program are still being finalized."
  • CFO Lind provided an expense framework rather than earnings guidance: "we expect quarterly SG&A for the core business to remain in a normalized range of approximately $10 million to $11 million. Excluding anticipated launch spend for the previously mentioned Medicare coverage program."

Financial Results

  • CFO Lind reported Q4 2025 consolidated net revenue of $13.3 million, describing it as "a strong sequential rebound of 15.8%" and "up 4.7% versus the prior year's $12.7 million in revenue," driven by "continued direct-to-consumer momentum" including "expanded sleep and functional gummy mushroom offerings, the Brightside low-dose hemp THC gummy line and new minor cannabinoid formulations."
  • CFO Lind reported Q4 gross profit of $5 million and gross margin of 37.5%, stating the margin was "significantly impacted by a nonrecurring $1.3 million inventory charge" that "reduced gross margin by about 10 percentage points," and added: "We expect gross margin to normalize toward our historical 50% range as we lap transitional items and as production efficiencies continue to scale."
  • CFO Lind reported Q4 SG&A of $10.6 million and cited "nonrecurring items" including "a $600,000 state sales tax audit accruals and certain contract termination and timing adjustments."
  • CFO Lind reported Q4 net loss of $11.4 million or $0.07 per share, and full-year 2025 net revenue of $49.9 million, which she said was "our first annual revenue increase since 2021."
  • CFO Lind reported full-year SG&A of $42 million and said cost actions "reduced annualized SG&A by approximately $33.6 million or 44.5% over the past 2 years." She also reported full-year net loss of $29.7 million or $0.19 per share and noted a "noncash change of $6.4 million in the fair value of the company's debt derivative and our investment in DeFloria."
  • CFO Lind reported liquidity and cash flow items including Q4 net cash used in operating activities of $1.9 million and year-end cash and working capital of $8 million and $21.7 million, adding the cash balance "does not reflect the BAT private placement, which adds $10 million in fresh capital."

Q&A

  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked what the participating centers are in the CMMI program and whether they are new setups; Chief People Officer Mindy Garrison answered: "these are not actually new facilities created for this program, they are established physician practices, health care systems, all combining together under an accountable care organization" and said initial models can begin "starting tomorrow, April 1."
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked who funds the $500 per patient per year and how access works; Chief People Officer Garrison said: "Medicare is not directly reimbursing these products" and that ACOs/EOMs "will purchase the eligible hemp-derived CBD products using its own funds," adding: "Charlotte's Web has built a portal" and products "would then be drop shipped to the patient's home," and "it's actually not a prescription. It's a recommendation from a health care provider."
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked for 2026 and 2027 revenue expectations from the pilot and whether Charlotte's Web is exclusive; CEO William Morachnick responded: "it's too premature to start modeling" and said, "there is no exclusivity," while arguing standards around "quality, around safety, around efficacy" should matter.
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked how the CMS program reconciles with a potential hemp ban effective November 12, 2026; CEO Morachnick said the current language could cap products at "0.4 milligrams of THC per container" and called out the mismatch versus CMS allowing "a 3-milligram THC cap per serving," adding the company is "working very closely" toward "a meaningful science-backed approach" and that leadership is "feeling good and confident that the Griffith's bill" aligns more with CMS guidance.
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked what drove the Q4 sequential sales growth and whether it is sustainable; CFO Erika Lind cited "continued D2C momentum," "targeted campaigns," B2B restructuring that removed "underperforming accounts," some retail customers moving to D2C, and "a strong holiday season."
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked about pro forma balance sheet strength, cash management, and CapEx; CFO Lind said the $10 million placement "significantly strengthens our liquidity" and emphasized that conversion eliminates "$55 million in the debt principle plus the $10 million in interest" and "prevents us from having to pay another $12 million for the balance of the note," adding the company will maintain the reduced cost structure while funding Medicare pilot launch costs.
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked why this was the right time for the BAT transaction; CFO Lind said "the opportunity drives the transaction" and cited needing to be "properly capitalized" for "the Medicare pilot programs" and the "DeFloria FDA pathway," while also discussing dilution and describing the private placement as "approximately 5% of post-conversion shares."
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked whether BAT can own more than 50%; CFO Lind said, "there is a part 49% cap of ownership" and anything beyond would be subject to "securities laws, TSX rules and also potentially shareholder approval."
  • Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates asked about FDA/OIRA CBD compliance headlines; CEO Morachnick declined to speculate: "it's too early and too speculative right now," and the call ended amid technical issues, with Investor Relations Cory Pala adding: "too soon to speculate" but "we are seeing a convergence of policy."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and probing, repeatedly pressing for quantification and regulatory clarity; Zuanic described "all the very positive news" but focused on uncertainties such as the hemp ban and FDA policy timing.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on regulatory outcomes; CEO Morachnick used high-conviction language such as "real catalysts unfolding now" while also stating on FDA/OIRA: "it's too early and too speculative right now."
  • Prior-quarter sentiment comparison could not be assessed because the previous-quarter transcript was not available in the prompt.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous-quarter transcript was not available in the prompt, so management guidance language shifts, changing analyst focus, and quarter-over-quarter tone comparisons could not be sourced.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Morachnick cited regulatory conflict risk between CMS guidance and pending federal legislative language, describing a potential cap at "0.4 milligrams of THC per container" as an outcome that "basically demolishes the CBD industry as we know it," while noting the company is engaging policymakers to seek "a consistency that can operate across the country."
  • Management highlighted execution risk in scaling the Medicare pilot, with CEO Morachnick saying education and practitioner comfort will take time, describing the ramp as "a gradual build."
  • CFO Lind flagged transaction completion risk requiring a shareholder vote: "the completion of this transaction requires approval from the majority of the shareholders" and stressed that "their vote matters."

Final Takeaway

Management presented the BAT conversion and $10 million private placement as a balance-sheet reset that removes the company’s largest liability while funding near-term initiatives, and positioned the CMMI Medicare pilot—starting April 1 with up to $500 per beneficiary annually funded by participating provider organizations—as an early-stage channel expected to build over the next 12 to 18 months, against a backdrop of unresolved federal hemp regulation and ongoing efforts to align policy with CMS’s allowance for up to 3 milligrams of THC per serving in eligible hemp-derived CBD products.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Shared Hospital Services outlines Bristol facility launch in late 2027, with Rhode Island proton center planned for 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Shared Hospital Services (ams) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Raymond Stachowiak framed 2025 as “a year of transition and investment,” saying total revenue “remained relatively stable at $28.1 million,” while AMS “continued our shift toward a direct patient care model, which now represents the majority of our revenue.” He cited “physician turnover, reimbursement dynamics and expected headwinds in our Leasing segment,” adding, “Importantly, we took decisive actions to address all these issues.”

  • Stachowiak highlighted partnership updates, including “our new collaboration with Brown University Health in Rhode Island,” and announced “our long-standing relationship with Orlando Health has been extended by a 7-year lease extension for our proton beam radiation therapy system.” He also said AMS is “actively engaged with our lending partners as we evaluate opportunities to enhance our capital structure,” calling discussions “constructive and ongoing.”

  • Chief Executive Officer Gary Delanois said 2025 “was a foundational year” as AMS expanded direct patient care services and “strengthened the operational infrastructure needed to support long-term growth.” He said Rhode Island physician staffing “has now been stabilized,” with “improvements in treatment volumes, which we expect to continue into 2026,” and pointed to “improving our revenue cycle management infrastructure” to gain “greater control over billing and collections.”

  • Chief Financial Officer Frech Scott said, “For the fourth quarter, total revenue decreased 14.8% to $7.7 million,” attributing the decline “primarily” to “the expiration of 3 Gamma Knife contracts and lower proton beam radiation therapy volumes,” while noting direct patient care represented “63% of total revenue.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide quantitative EPS or revenue guidance. Delanois described priorities as: “increase treatment volumes across our existing centers, drive operational efficiencies and margin improvement, expand our footprint through disciplined development and continue to leverage our partnerships to scale our platform.”

  • Delanois gave a construction timeline for Rhode Island expansion in Q&A: “We anticipate that the Bristol facility will come online in late '27 and followed by the proton facility in '28.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter call, management’s forward-looking language shifted from Q3’s “we expect to start treating patients and generating revenue in the second quarter of 2026” (for Guadalajara) to Q4’s broader statement that Guadalajara “we expect to begin operations in 2026,” without reaffirming the specific quarter.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $7.7 million. Scott reported segment detail: direct patient care revenue “increasing 2.6% year-over-year to $4.8 million,” while leasing revenue “declined 33.9% to $2.9 million.”

  • Profitability and cash metrics in Q4 included: “Gross margin for the quarter was approximately $906,000 or 12%,” “Net loss attributable to the company improved to $631,000 or $0.09 per diluted share,” and “Adjusted EBITDA was $868,000.”

  • Full-year 2025 results reflected mix shift and lower margins: Scott reported “total revenue was $28.1 million,” with direct patient care services revenue “increased 23.7% to $15.5 million,” while leasing revenue “declined to $12.6 million.” He also reported modality revenue: “LINAC revenue increased 35.4% to $11.5 million,” “Gamma Knife revenue decreased 5.5% to $9.2 million,” and “proton beam radiation therapy revenue declined 26% to $7.4 million.”

  • Cash, debt, and covenant disclosures tightened focus on financing risk. Scott said AMS ended 2025 with “approximately $3.7 million in cash,” “total debt… approximately $17.3 million,” and that “certain financial covenants were not met at year-end.” He added: “While these conditions raise substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern if unresolved, we are confident in our path forward based on our ongoing lender engagement and improved operational performance.”

Q&A

  • Marla Marin, Zacks Small-Cap Research asked whether Rhode Island’s expanding footprint can offset upgrade-related “noise” by shifting patients across centers; Chief Executive Officer Delanois responded, “that is certainly part of the strategy in Rhode Island,” citing the ability to “leverage that infrastructure over a bigger footprint” and “economies of scale.”

  • Marin, Zacks Small-Cap Research asked about the Bristol construction timeline and staffing lead time; Delanois said Bristol is expected “late '27” and proton “'28,” and added, “we normally start staffing up several months in advance,” using shared staffing across sites so AMS can “very closely manage our expenses.”

  • Anthony Marchese (private investor) questioned disclosure of “the 3 expired contracts,” asking why investors are “constantly surprised”; Stachowiak replied, “We've mentioned it in past calls and disclosures,” and explained quarterly variance timing effects as contracts roll off comparison periods.

  • Marchese asked whether AMS anticipates profitability in 2026; Stachowiak said, “we really can't speculate on that,” adding, “we really have not ever been in the habit of giving forward-looking statements.”

  • Marchese challenged board alignment and asked about stock buybacks amid comments about trading at a “steep discount”; Stachowiak responded, “the company has not really been interested in a buyback -- stock buyback program,” and added, “under this situation with our lenders, it's unlikely to change that stance,” while saying, “I remain very bullish on the company.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to negative in spots, led by Marchese’s pressing questions on contract disclosure, profitability, buybacks, and board ownership: “I'm sorry, I have to be so harsh, but as an investor, I like to see the Board aligned with investors.”

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more constrained/defensive in Q&A on forward-looking profitability and capital actions, including Stachowiak’s, “we really can't speculate on that,” and buyback stance being “unlikely to change.”

  • Versus Q3, where analysts were largely supportive (including “congratulations” and urging outreach), Q4 featured sharper skepticism on governance and communication, while management’s confidence language remained present (Delanois: “we are optimistic about 2026 and confident in our long-term growth trajectory”) alongside heightened balance-sheet caution.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The strategic narrative stayed consistent around the shift to direct patient care, but Q4 emphasized operational disruption and mix pressure (Q4 gross margin “12%” vs. Q3 gross margin “22.1%”) and introduced explicit going-concern language tied to covenant breaches.

  • In Q3, management highlighted sequential improvements and strong startup momentum (Puebla and Rhode Island ramp), and investor discussions centered on valuation and outreach; in Q4, the discussion shifted to contract expirations, reduced leasing revenue, lender negotiations, and buyback constraints.

  • Timelines remained focused on Rhode Island buildout, with Q4 adding specificity for Bristol (“late '27”) and proton (“'28”), while Guadalajara timing became less specific than Q3’s “second quarter of 2026.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Covenant noncompliance and liquidity were the most explicit financial risks. Scott said “certain financial covenants were not met,” and warned of “substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern if unresolved,” while stating AMS is in “active and constructive discussions with our lender regarding amendments and potential restructuring.”

  • Operational risks cited by Stachowiak included “physician turnover,” “reimbursement dynamics,” and “expected headwinds in our Leasing segment,” with mitigation described as “decisive actions” plus physician stabilization in Rhode Island and revenue-cycle improvements.

  • Commercial risk in leasing was highlighted through “the expiration of 3 Gamma Knife contracts,” and management stated the customers chose to “do the update themselves rather than utilize us.”

Final Takeaway

Management described 2025 as a transition year toward direct patient care, pairing partnership-driven expansion plans—such as Bristol coming online in late 2027 and a Rhode Island proton center in 2028—with near-term pressure from contract expirations, lower proton volumes, and a weaker margin profile. The most stock-sensitive development was the disclosure of year-end covenant breaches and the statement that unresolved issues “raise substantial doubt” about going-concern status, while management emphasized ongoing lender negotiations and maintained that a stock buyback is unlikely, particularly under current lender conditions.
